text.title: Strategy, Observer, and Command Patterns
text.summary: When-to/when-NOT rules, modern implementation trends, and selection triggers for Strategy (interchangeable algorithms), Observer (one-to-many notification), and Command (encapsulated operations with undo/redo).

rule.statement: Apply Strategy when you have three or more algorithm variants that swap at runtime AND the switching logic is a growing if/else or switch-on-type. With two variants, a simple conditional is cleaner.
rule.rationale: Premature abstraction with one or two implementations produces an interface with no real polymorphism; the Strategy indirection adds ceremony without extensibility benefit.

list.item: Use when: multiple algorithms for the same task selectable at runtime (payment methods, sorting, validation, compression), or you want to isolate algorithm implementation from consuming code.
Avoid when: only 2–3 simple algorithms unlikely to change, or clients have no reason to know about alternative strategies.
Modern Python: use Protocol for duck typing — no abstract base class required. The strategy is any object with the right method signature.
Modern TypeScript: discriminated unions provide type-safe strategy selection at compile time. Strategy as a function type (not a class) is idiomatic for single-method strategies.
Dependency injection frameworks (Spring, FastAPI, NestJS): strategies are injected as constructor parameters, making the pattern implicit and testable without factory boilerplate.

rule.statement: Use Observer for fan-out notification to N consumers where the subject does not need to know who is listening. Use direct callbacks or dependency injection when the relationship is one-to-one.
rule.rationale: Observer decouples the subject from consumers at the cost of making notification order and error propagation implicit. One-to-one relationships don't need that indirection.

list.item: Use when: objects need notification of state changes, implementing event-driven architectures, building reactive systems.
Avoid when: simple one-to-one relationships, notification order is critical (use an ordered event chain instead), or you need synchronous error propagation from observers.
Python: WeakSet for observers prevents memory leaks when observer objects are garbage-collected. subscribe() returns an unsubscribe callable.
TypeScript: typed EventEmitter with generic event map provides compile-time checking of event names and payload shapes.

Modern trends: reactive streams (RxJS, RxPy), Signals in Solid/Angular/Vue (fine-grained reactivity without explicit subscribe/unsubscribe). Event sourcing integrates Observer at the architectural level.

antipattern.description: Attaching observers without an unsubscribe mechanism in long-lived objects.
antipattern.reason: Memory leak: the subject holds a strong reference to the observer, preventing garbage collection. Always return an unsubscribe function or use WeakSet/WeakRef.

rule.statement: Apply Command when you need undo/redo, operation queuing, or audit logging — not as a general wrapper for every method call. Command adds a class per operation; reserve it for operations where that overhead is justified.
rule.rationale: Wrapping every service method in a Command object creates an explosion of classes with no real benefit if the operations never need to be undone, queued, or replayed.

list.item: Use when: undo/redo functionality, queuing operations for later or out-of-order execution, logging/auditing all actions, implementing transactions or sagas.
Avoid when: simple operations without undo requirements, no need for operation history.
Invoker pattern: an Invoker holds a history stack and redo stack. execute() pushes to history and clears redo; undo() pops from history and pushes to redo.

Modern trends: CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) separates write commands from read queries at the architecture level. Event sourcing stores commands as the source of truth. Saga orchestration in microservices uses Command objects to coordinate distributed transactions.
